original_text: You publish a popular book on Amazon.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: You publish a book on Amazon that goes entirely unnoticed, selling almost no copies.

variations.label: low
variations.text: You publish a little-known book on Amazon that attracts only a small handful of readers.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: You publish a moderately successful book on Amazon with a steady but modest readership.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: You publish a popular book on Amazon.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: You publish a bestselling book on Amazon that ranks highly in its category.

variations.label: high
variations.text: You publish a #1 bestselling book on Amazon that dominates the charts for months.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: You publish a book on Amazon that becomes an all-time bestselling global phenomenon, read by millions worldwide.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier represents a meaningfully distinct level of popularity/success: from virtually zero readership (tier 1), through small (tier 2), modest (tier 3), popular (tier 4), bestselling (tier 5), chart-dominating (tier 6), to all-time global phenomenon (tier 7). The core scenario—publishing a book on Amazon—is preserved throughout, with only the degree of success varying.

preference_direction: Higher tiers are more preferable because greater popularity and success for one's published book means more readers, more cultural impact, more recognition, and more financial reward—all outcomes a rational agent would prefer over lesser success.
